explicit_text: I often catch pieces of the Mississippi film A Time to Kill, which came out in 1996. It was filmed on location in Canton, Madison County, a place with a deeply racist history that I know well from my own experiences dating back to 1961. The film’s story about a young lawyer protecting his family during a tense trial reminded me of my own life, especially a poignant scene where the lawyer sends his wife and child away for safety, just like I did with Eldri and Baby Maria.

explicit_text: John Grisham wrote the novel that A Time to Kill was based on, and I can’t help but wonder if he read my book. The film adaptation was shot in Canton, a place I know intimately from the civil rights struggles of the early 1960s. Grisham’s story about legal battles and racial tensions resonates with my own experiences during the Movement in Mississippi.
implicit_text: The author of the novel that inspired the 1996 Mississippi film likely knew the civil rights struggles of the early 1960s in Canton, a place I know intimately. I can’t help but wonder if he read my book. His story about legal battles and racial tensions resonates with my own experiences during the Movement in Mississippi.

explicit_text: Canton, in Madison County just north of Jackson, was one of the worst racist settings during the Bad Old Days. I have many stories from Canton, some going back to 1961, and it was the place where I went to get a license plate for our new car in 1963. The old white courthouse there, featured in A Time to Kill, was where I faced Sheriff Billy Noble and Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen, two of the worst racist officials in the state.
implicit_text: This town in Madison County just north of Jackson was one of the worst racist settings during the Bad Old Days. I have many stories from there, some going back to 1961, and it was the place where I went to get a license plate for our new car in 1963. The old white courthouse there, featured in the 1996 film, was where I faced Sheriff Billy Noble and Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen, two of the worst racist officials in the state.

explicit_text: Madison County, where Canton is located, was part of the complex geography of Tougaloo College’s campus, half of which lies there. In early July 1963, Eldri and I drove to Canton in Madison County to get a license plate for our new car, an act that put me face-to-face with hostile local law enforcement. This county was a significant part of the civil rights struggle I lived through.

explicit_text: Jackson was the place where I was targeted in a rigged car wreck on June 18, 1963, during the height of the civil rights Movement. It was also where McKay Motors, a white-owned business that refused to support the White Citizens' Council, sold me a new Rambler after my old car was totaled. Jackson was central to many events in my life, including the injunction served by Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen that we defied.

explicit_text: Mississippi was the battleground for much of my civil rights activism in the early 1960s. From Canton to Jackson and Tougaloo College, I experienced firsthand the entrenched racism and violent opposition to the Movement. The White Citizens' Council and officials like Sheriff Billy Noble embodied the hostile environment we faced, but we persisted in our struggle for justice.
implicit_text: This southern state was the battleground for much of my civil rights activism in the early 1960s. From that town to the capital city and Tougaloo College, I experienced firsthand the entrenched racism and violent opposition to the Movement. The White Citizens' Council and officials like Sheriff Billy Noble embodied the hostile environment we faced, but we persisted in our struggle for justice.

explicit_text: In June 1963, I sent Eldri and our daughter Maria to Minnesota to stay with her parents because of constant threats and bomb scares we were facing in Mississippi. Eldri was reluctant to leave but I insisted it was necessary for their safety. Maria stayed there with her grandparents while I recovered from a near-fatal car wreck in Jackson.
implicit_text: In June 1963, I sent Eldri and our daughter Maria to the northern Midwestern state to stay with her parents because of constant threats and bomb scares we were facing in the southern state. Eldri was reluctant to leave but I insisted it was necessary for their safety. Maria stayed there with her grandparents while I recovered from a near-fatal car wreck in the southern city.

explicit_text: Tougaloo College was my home and base of operations during the civil rights Movement. After my car wreck in June 1963, I returned there to recover, living on campus in the Madison County portion of the grounds. It was also where I received the new Rambler from McKay Motors and where Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen came with armed deputies to serve the anti-Movement injunction.
implicit_text: This historically black college was my home and base of operations during the civil rights Movement. After my car wreck in June 1963, I returned there to recover, living on campus in the Madison County portion of the grounds. It was also where I received the new Rambler from the supportive white-owned dealership and where Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen came with armed deputies to serve the anti-Movement injunction.

explicit_text: McKay Motors in Jackson was one of the few white-owned businesses we did not boycott because they refused to support the White Citizens' Council. After my car was wrecked in June 1963, a salesman from McKay Motors came to Tougaloo College with a brand-new Rambler for me to sign for on credit. Their support was a rare bright spot in a hostile environment.

explicit_text: Medgar Evers was deeply knowledgeable about the racial and political scene in Mississippi and informed me that McKay Motors had repeatedly declined to contribute to White Citizens' Council causes. He also loaned me a Winchester Model 1873 rifle, which I considered my life insurance policy during those dangerous times. Medgar’s support and insight were invaluable during the Movement.

explicit_text: The White Citizens' Council was a powerful racist organization in Mississippi that many white businesses supported. McKay Motors was notable for refusing to contribute to their causes, which made them an exception in Jackson. The Council represented the entrenched opposition we faced during the civil rights Movement.
implicit_text: This powerful racist organization in the southern state was supported by many white businesses. explicit_text: The Winchester Model 1873 rifle was loaned to me by Medgar Evers some weeks before my car wreck in 1963. I told the McKay Motors salesman that this rifle was my life insurance policy, acknowledging the very real dangers I faced as a civil rights activist in Mississippi. It was a symbol of the constant threat and need for protection in those times.

explicit_text: I was a member of the American Federation of Teachers through Phoenix Local 1010 on an at-large basis. Bill Karnes, the president and national vice-president of AFT, arranged for me to have a life insurance policy, which was another form of protection during the dangerous civil rights era. This support was important alongside the physical protection I maintained.

His role exemplified the solidarity some organizations showed to activists like me.

explicit_text: Hinds County is where half of Tougaloo College and its grounds are located, including the part in Jackson. It was also the jurisdiction of the Hinds County Chancery Court, which issued the sweeping anti-Movement injunction that Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen served on me and others. This county was a significant legal battleground during the civil rights era.

entity: City of Jackson v. John R. Salter, Jr.
explicit_text: The case City of Jackson v. John R. Salter, Jr. was an anti-Movement injunction issued by the Hinds County Chancery Court in 1963. Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen came to Tougaloo College with armed deputies to serve me and others with this sweeping injunction, which we defiantly ignored. This legal action was part of the broader efforts to suppress civil rights activism in Jackson.

explicit_text: Billy Noble was the Sheriff of Madison County and one of the worst racist officials in Mississippi. I encountered him in the Canton courthouse in July 1963 when I went to get a license plate for our new car. His presence, along with Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen, was intimidating, but I remained calm and defiant in the face of their hostility.
implicit_text: The sheriff of the county where that town is located was one of the worst racist officials in the southern state. I encountered him in the courthouse there in July 1963 when I went to get a license plate for our new car. His presence, along with Chief Deputy Jack Cauthen, was intimidating, but I remained calm and defiant in the face of their hostility.

explicit_text: Jack Cauthen was the Chief Deputy in Madison County and a staunch opponent of the civil rights Movement. A month before I went to Canton courthouse, he had come with heavily armed deputies to Tougaloo College to serve me and others with the anti-Movement injunction. When I later faced him and Sheriff Noble at the courthouse, he smirked as he handed me the papers, but I stood my ground without flinching.

explicit_text: The Sierra Peaks are a mountain range in north central Arizona, and a few lines from an old cowboy ballad about them came to mind when I faced Sheriff Noble and Chief Deputy Cauthen in the Canton courthouse. The ballad’s line about the Devil riding down the draw captured the surreal and dangerous moment perfectly. It reminded me of my Arizona roots and the gravity of the situation in Mississippi.

explicit_text: Arizona is my home state, and it shaped much of my character and perspective. The little lady at the Canton courthouse reminded me of someone from Arizona, and I even used a Northern Arizona drawl when I thanked her after getting the license plate. The old cowboy ballad about the Sierra Peaks in Arizona also came to mind during tense moments in Mississippi, linking my past to the present struggle.
